content.text: Every modern browser ships a built-in developer tools suite. For Angular applications these tools expose the rendered component DOM, HTTP traffic, JavaScript execution, storage state, and performance profiles — all without installing anything beyond the browser itself.

attrs.alt: Browser DevTools panels for Angular: Elements, Console, Network, Sources, Application, Angular DevTools extension

content.text: The six panels above cover the main debugging workflows. The purple Angular DevTools panel is a separate Chrome/Firefox extension maintained by the Angular team that adds component-tree inspection and a change-detection profiler on top of the built-in tools.
(macOS) — open DevTools in its last position.
— open DevTools with the Console panel active.
— open DevTools in element-inspection mode (cursor turns into a picker).
Right-click any element on the page and choose
— opens DevTools with that element selected in Elements.

DevTools opens as a separate window or docked to the bottom or side of the browser. Docking preference is saved per origin. Undocking is useful on wide monitors so the DevTools window can be dragged to a second screen.

The Elements panel shows the live DOM tree produced by Angular's renderer. Angular component tags (app-root, app-header, etc.) appear directly in the tree, which makes it easy to verify that the right components rendered and in the right order.
Inspecting Angular Components in the DOM
Click any element to select it. The Styles pane on the right shows all CSS rules applying to it, including Angular's component-scoped styles (identified by the _ngcontent-* attribute selector).
The selected element is assigned to
in the Console, so you can type
in the Console and call methods on it immediately.
Use the Computed sub-panel to see the final box-model dimensions — useful when diagnosing layout issues in Angular Material or Flex Layout components.
to test styles that only apply during interaction.

Click any CSS property value in the Styles pane to edit it. Changes are applied immediately without a page reload. This is the fastest way to prototype style adjustments before committing them to your .scss files. Double-click a selector to add new declarations.
The Console is the primary place for runtime debugging. Angular exposes a global ng object in development mode that gives direct access to the component tree.
The Angular component instance attached to the selected element
The component that owns a given element (useful inside ng-template)
The template context for a given node (e.g. NgFor local variables)
Array of directives applied to the selected element
Triggers change detection on the component — use after mutating a property
The injector for the element — call .get(ServiceClass) to access services
These helpers only exist in development builds. A production build (ng build --configuration production) removes them entirely, so they will not be available on deployed sites.
Use the filter box at the top to search console output by text. Type
to see only network-logged requests, or
Click the level buttons (Verbose, Info, Warnings, Errors) to show or hide specific severities.
Prefix your own log calls with a group name —
The Network panel records every HTTP request the browser makes. For Angular applications built on HttpClient, this panel shows all API calls, their timing, response bodies, and headers.
Use the filter bar to type a URL fragment — e.g.,
— to show only your backend requests and hide asset loads.
button to limit the list to data requests (hides scripts, styles, images).
Click any request row to open the detail panel: Headers (method, status, request/response headers), Preview (parsed JSON), Response (raw body), Timing (DNS + TCP + TTFB + Download breakdown).

The throttling dropdown (next to the filter bar) lets you emulate a 3G or 4G connection. This reveals Angular lazy loading performance — you can see which chunks are loaded on route change and how long each takes to download.

Network waterfall example for ng serve with lazy routes:
  GET /                    200  HTML    45ms
  GET /main.js             200  script  312ms
  GET /polyfills.js        200  script  28ms
  --- user navigates to /products route ---
  GET /products.chunk.js   200  script  89ms   ← lazy-loaded route chunk
  GET /api/products        200  fetch   142ms  ← HttpClient request
in the Network panel toolbar to keep all requests visible across page navigation and Angular router transitions. Without this, the log clears every time Angular performs a hard navigation.
The Sources panel lets you set breakpoints directly in your TypeScript source files (not the compiled JavaScript) thanks to source maps generated by the Angular CLI.
on macOS) inside the Sources panel to open a file by name — type a partial file name like
If TypeScript files do not appear, check that
Click a line number in a .ts file to set a breakpoint (blue marker). The browser pauses on that line the next time it executes.
Trigger the code path in the Angular app — navigate to a route, click a button, or call a service method.
The browser pauses execution. The Scope panel on the right shows all local variables and their current values.
(Shift+F11) to navigate the call stack one statement at a time.
The Call Stack panel shows the complete call chain, including Angular internal frames — look for your component or service name to find the relevant frame.
Right-click a line number and choose Add Conditional Breakpoint to enter a JavaScript expression. The debugger only pauses when the expression is truthy:
The Performance panel records a timeline of CPU activity, rendering frames, and JavaScript execution. Use it to diagnose slow Angular rendering or excessive change-detection cycles.
Click the Record button (circle) or press Ctrl+E.
Interact with the Angular app — scroll a large list, open a dialog, trigger a route transition.
Click Stop. The panel renders a flame chart of JavaScript execution.
Look for wide orange bars labelled
runs its check on every cycle, even when its inputs haven't changed. In the flame chart this appears as repeated short calls to
Long tasks (>50ms) are highlighted in red. These block the main thread and cause frame drops.
The Frames track at the top shows rendering frames. Frames shorter than 16.7ms (60 fps) are green; longer frames are yellow or red.
The Application panel exposes client-side storage that Angular apps commonly use: Local Storage, Session Storage, Cookies, and IndexedDB (often used by NgRx Store's persistence adapters).
Persisted auth tokens, user preferences, theme settings
Per-tab state (e.g. multi-step form in progress)
HttpOnly session cookies set by the server, CSRF tokens
NgRx store persistence, offline data caches
Angular Service Worker (PWA) caches for offline support
Click any storage entry to see its contents. You can add, edit, or delete entries directly in the panel — useful for testing how the Angular app reacts to missing or malformed tokens.
Install the Angular DevTools extension from the Chrome Web Store (also available for Firefox). It adds an Angular tab to DevTools that provides two features not available in built-in panels:
Shows the Angular component hierarchy — not just the DOM tag names but the actual class names and change-detection strategy.
Select a component to see its @Input properties, their current values, and the component's @Output event emitters.
Clicking on a component in the tree selects it as
in the Console, so you can call
Record a profiling session identical to the Performance panel but filtered to Angular's change-detection cycles only.
Each bar represents one CD cycle. Hover to see which component triggered it and how long it took.
are immediately visible as high-frequency entries — a direct pointer to where to optimise.
Angular DevTools only works with Angular 9+ applications running in development mode (ng serve without --configuration production). It has no effect on production builds.
Reproduce the action in the Angular app that triggers the request.
Click the failed request (red row) → Headers tab → note the status code and URL.
Check the Response tab for the error body returned by the server.
In the Console, catch the error: Angular's HttpClient logs uncaught errors to console by default. Add
in the service if it is being swallowed.
Diagnosing an Expression that Doesn't Update
Click the element whose binding is not updating in Elements →
If the property has the correct value but the DOM has not updated, the component is likely using OnPush and change detection has not run.
to manually trigger it and confirm.
Fix: ensure that mutable array/object changes go through a new reference (
Memory Panel — Detecting Leaks in Angular

Angular components that subscribe to Observables without unsubscribing hold references to services and DOM elements even after the component is destroyed. The Memory panel's Heap Snapshot comparison is the most reliable way to detect these leaks.
In the app: navigate to the component you suspect is leaking, then navigate away so it should be destroyed.
Select the second snapshot and use the
view to see objects created since the baseline. Look for instances of your component class name — they should be 0 if the component was properly destroyed.
Common Angular Memory Leaks and Fixes
Component class instance lingers after destroy
Global event listeners added in constructor
Listener holds a closure over the component

The Lighthouse panel (available in Chrome) runs a comprehensive audit of your Angular app and reports scores for Performance, Accessibility, Best Practices, and SEO. For an Angular app, the most actionable Lighthouse findings are:
Large initial bundle — all routes eagerly loaded
Add lazy loading to feature routes
Long JavaScript tasks on initial load
Images without explicit width/height; skeleton screens missing
Set explicit dimensions on img elements
Angular app not installable as PWA

Run Lighthouse in Incognito mode to avoid interference from browser extensions. Use Throttling: Slow 4G and CPU 4× slowdown to simulate a mid-range mobile device — this reveals bundle size problems that are invisible on a developer machine.
Right-click a request URL in the Network panel and choose

. This simulates the API being unavailable. Reload the page or trigger the Angular route to see how your app handles the missing endpoint — does the error propagate to the template? Does the Angular error boundary catch it?
In Chrome 117+, right-click a request and choose

. DevTools saves a local copy of the response that replaces the real network response on subsequent requests. This is useful for testing Angular components with edge-case API payloads (empty arrays, very large datasets, or malformed JSON) without modifying the backend.

Click the download icon in the Network toolbar to export a HAR (HTTP Archive) file. Share it with colleagues or open it in online HAR viewers to reproduce a network recording. Useful when you cannot reproduce a production bug locally — have users capture a HAR with DevTools and send it to you.

Press Ctrl+Shift+M (Cmd+Shift+M) to toggle the Device Toolbar. This emulates a mobile viewport and disables hover states, which is important for testing Angular responsive layouts and touch-event handling.
Select a preset device (iPhone 14, Pixel 7) or enter a custom width to test
breakpoints in your Angular component styles.
Enable "Show media queries" in the three-dot menu to visualise breakpoint lines across the viewport ruler.
Touch simulation: when in device mode, click events are converted to touch events. Test Angular CDK drag-and-drop or Hammer.js gesture directives here.
Throttle CPU and network independently from the device mode toggle to isolate the effect of each on Core Web Vitals.

Browser DevTools are the primary runtime debugging environment for Angular applications. The Elements panel shows the live component DOM; the Console provides the ng.* helpers to inspect component state without breakpoints; the Network panel tracks every HttpClient call; the Sources panel enables TypeScript breakpoints via source maps; the Performance panel locates slow change-detection cycles; and the Application panel exposes client-side storage. The Angular DevTools extension adds a component-tree inspector and a change-detection profiler that make Angular-specific performance work considerably faster.
